title: practical english usage - Part 215
content:
For singular or plural verbs after expressions with and or or, ►     130.4.

Daniel started first, (and) then Tom (started).

and is dropped.

In an informal style, ellipsis is sometimes possible after then, even if 4 (and)    then (e.g. if possible, when arriving), ►    115.6, 244.6, 251.5.

However, ellipsis of subject pronouns with forms of be is possible in some cases not She didn't know where she was when she woke up. (     .. .-when woke up.) Ellipsis is not normally possible after other conjunctions besides and, but and or.

3 other     conjunctions You seem, and she certainly is, ill.

Megan went to Greece and Alice to Rome.

The children will carry the small boxes and the adults the large ones.

Annie likes athletics.) Kevin likes dancing and Annie athletics. (Less formal: Kevin likes dancing and trip and Megan paid for it.) Daniel planned and Megan paid for the trip. (Less formal: Daniel planned the more formal style.

word order of a clause or sentence. Sentences like the following are typical of a In informal speech and writing, ellipsis does not usually interrupt the normal I can (go) and will go.

Cats (catch mice) and dogs catch mice, (     Getis-eateh-miee-and-deg^.) produce a simpler word order and sentence structure.

that is left out We   may have to leave out the first to avoid confusion, or to Note that when two verbs, objects, etc are the same, it is not always the second 2 word    order Information StructureSection 25 grammar •  278  ellipsis in noun phrases ‘ That beer went down fast.' ‘ Have another (beer).’ ‘ Can I borrow your pen? ‘ No, find your own (pen).' You take Dan's car, and I'll take Susie's (car).

Our  train's the second (train) from this platform.

I'm not sure how many packets I need, but I'll take two (packets) to start with.

This also happens after numbers, nouns with possessive 's, own and (an)other.

This is Ella's coat, and that (coat) is mine.

meaning is clear.

Nouns can also be dropped after most determiners (►  Sections 12-13),  if the 2 ellipsis     after    this,    numbers,      possessives,        etc For other structures in which  adjectives are used without nouns, ►     188.

Z think I'll buy the cheapest.

This often happens after superlatives.

We haven't got any brown sugar. Only white (sugar).

‘ What kind of potatoes would you like?' ‘ Boiled (potatoes), please.' clear, especially when one is talking about common kinds of choice.

A  repeated noun can sometimes be dropped after an adjective, if the meaning is 1  ellipsis     after    adjectives:       boiled,       please 278 ellip   sis   in  noun    p hrases    _____     ______ Getting in your way, am I?  Going camping your kids?

Can't swim, myself Like a cigar, I do. Dutch, aren't you?

►    299) on the end, especially in British English.

Ellipsis is very common in sentences that have some sort of tag (►  305-306, 4 Dutch,      aren     't you     ?

She want something? (= Does she want something?) You ready? (= Are you ready?) Auxiliary verbs can be left out before personal pronouns except I and it.

3 before     pronouns:      You     ready?

Haven't seen him. (          Have seen him. Have is not stressed.) or                                   not I'll see you soon,  See you soon, (     Will see you seen. Will is not stressed.) Am  is not stressed.) or                                                 not I'm coming tomorrow,  Coming tomorrow, (     Am-coming-tomorrew: diary entries and other kinds of very informal writing).

be, will, would or auxiliary have (though this sometimes happens in messages, We   do not usually drop words so as to begin sentences with unstressed forms of 2 unstressed        forms     of  be,   will,    would,       have Speak a foreign language? Speak it better. (= Do  you speak ...?) Thinking of postgraduate study? Call for a place now. (= Are you thinking ...?) This structure is common in advertisements. Two   real examples: Soon as I wake up, I check my messages. (= As soon as ...) Leave at half past? (= Should we  leave ...?) Catch up with you later. (= I’ ll catch up ...) grammar •  279  ellipsis after auxiliary verbs You don't want to buy a car, do you?
